definition: Divides the body into left and right halves. Movement occurs in a forward and backward direction (flexion and extension).
example: Running, jumping, squatting, and cycling all occur in the sagittal plane.

definition: Divides the body into front and back halves. Movement occurs side to side (abduction and adduction).
example: A star jump, side shuffle, or a gymnast performing a cartwheel move in the frontal plane.

definition: Divides the body into upper and lower halves. Rotational movements occur in this plane.
example: A golf swing, discus throw, or body rotation in swimming all occur in the transverse plane.

definition: An object at rest will remain at rest, and an object in motion will remain in motion at the same speed and direction, unless acted upon by an external force.
example: A football remains still until kicked (external force); once kicked it continues moving until friction/air resistance acts on it.

definition: Force = Mass × Acceleration (F = ma). A greater force applied to the same mass produces greater acceleration.
example: A heavier shot putt requires more force to throw the same distance as a lighter one.

definition: For every action there is an equal and opposite reaction.
example: A swimmer pushes water backwards (action); the water pushes the swimmer forward (reaction).

definition: The ability of the heart, lungs, and circulatory system to supply oxygen and nutrients to working muscles during prolonged physical activity.
example: A 5 km run develops cardiovascular endurance. Improved CV endurance reduces risk of heart disease and stroke.

definition: The maximum force a muscle or muscle group can exert in a single contraction/effort.
example: Weight training (deadlifts, bench press) develops muscular strength. Benefits include improved posture and reduced injury risk.

definition: The ability of a muscle or group of muscles to perform repeated contractions against a resistance over time without fatiguing.
example: Circuit training and swimming develop muscular endurance — essential for sports like rowing and cycling.

definition: The range of motion available at a joint. Depends on muscle length, joint structure, and connective tissue elasticity.
example: Yoga and static stretching improve flexibility. Reduces injury risk and muscle soreness (DOMS).

definition: The proportion of lean body mass (muscle, bone, organs) to fat mass in the body. Usually expressed as % body fat.
example: A combination of cardio and resistance training improves body composition, reducing risk of obesity-related diseases.

definition: A structured warm-up framework: Raise (heart rate and body temperature), Activate (key muscle groups), Mobilise (joints through full ROM), Potentiate (sport-specific, high-intensity preparation).
example: Before GAA training: light jog (Raise) → glute bridges (Activate) → hip circles (Mobilise) → sprints (Potentiate).

definition: The body must be trained beyond its normal workload to cause physiological adaptations and improvements in fitness.
example: A runner increases their weekly mileage from 30 km to 35 km to overload their cardiovascular system.

definition: Training must match the specific demands of the sport, the energy systems used, and the muscle groups involved.
example: A 100 m sprinter trains with short explosive intervals — not long slow runs — because sprinting is anaerobic and speed-focused.

definition: Fitness gains are lost if training stops or is significantly reduced. 'Use it or lose it.'
example: An athlete recovering from injury loses cardiovascular fitness within 2–4 weeks of inactivity.

definition: Training load must be gradually and systematically increased over time to continue stimulating adaptations without causing overtraining or injury.
example: A beginner starts with 2 training sessions per week and gradually increases to 4 over 3 months.

definition: A training method that alternates periods of high-intensity exercise with periods of rest or low-intensity recovery. Develops anaerobic capacity and speed.
example: 10 × 200 m sprints at near-maximum effort with 90 seconds' rest — used by GAA or athletics sprinters.

definition: Sustained aerobic exercise at a moderate intensity for a prolonged period (typically 20+ minutes) without rest intervals. Develops cardiovascular endurance.
example: A 45-minute run at 65–75% maximum heart rate — used by distance runners or cyclists to build aerobic base.

definition: The internal and external factors that initiate, direct, and sustain behaviour in sport. Intrinsic motivation comes from within (enjoyment, personal challenge); extrinsic motivation comes from outside (medals, praise).
example: An athlete who trains because they love the sport has intrinsic motivation; one who trains for prize money has extrinsic motivation.

definition: Setting Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound goals to enhance motivation, focus, and performance direction.
example: Goal: 'Run a 5 km in under 25 minutes within 8 weeks' — this is SMART, unlike 'get fitter'.

definition: Arousal is physiological and psychological activation. Too low = poor focus; too high = anxiety. The Inverted-U theory suggests optimal performance occurs at a moderate arousal level.
example: A golfer performs best at moderate arousal — over-arousal (nerves) leads to muscle tension and missed putts.

definition: Self-efficacy is the belief in one's ability to successfully perform a specific task. High self-efficacy leads to greater effort, persistence, and performance.
example: A basketball player who believes they can make the free throw (high self-efficacy) is more likely to succeed under pressure.

definition: Creating a mental picture of successfully performing a skill or competition. Activates the same neural pathways as physical practice and builds confidence.
example: A gymnast mentally rehearses their floor routine before competition — visualising each skill successfully executed.

definition: A negative emotional state associated with nervousness and worry. Somatic anxiety: physical symptoms (sweating, increased heart rate). Cognitive anxiety: negative thoughts and worry.
example: Before a penalty shoot-out, a player may experience cognitive anxiety (fear of missing) and somatic anxiety (shaky legs).

definition: Produces ATP using oxygen for prolonged, lower-intensity activity. Uses glucose, fat, and sometimes protein. Produces CO₂ and water as by-products.
example: Running a marathon relies almost entirely on the aerobic system. It produces ~36 ATP per glucose molecule.

definition: Produce ATP without oxygen. ATP-PC system (explosive, 0–10 secs): uses creatine phosphate. Lactic acid system (30 secs–2 min): glucose → pyruvate → lactic acid.
example: A 100 m sprint uses the ATP-PC system; an 800 m race uses the lactic acid system; a marathon uses aerobic.

definition: Lactic acid builds up when oxygen can't meet energy demand during high-intensity exercise. EPOC (Excess Post-Exercise Oxygen Consumption) is the increased oxygen uptake after exercise to repay the 'oxygen debt' and restore homeostasis.
example: After a 400 m race, heavy breathing continues for several minutes — this is EPOC in action.

definition: The maximum volume of oxygen a person can use per minute per kilogram of body weight during maximal exercise. A key measure of aerobic fitness.
example: Elite endurance athletes like Tour de France cyclists have VO₂ max values of 80–90 mL/kg/min — trained for higher O₂ delivery and extraction.

definition: The standard first aid treatment for soft tissue injuries: Rest (stop activity), Ice (apply for 15–20 min), Compression (bandage to reduce swelling), Elevation (raise above heart level).
example: A sprained ankle on the pitch: immediately stop play (Rest), apply ice pack (Ice), bandage firmly (Compression), elevate on a bag (Elevation).

definition: A learned ability to bring about predetermined results with maximum certainty, minimum energy, and minimum time.
example: A tennis serve is a closed skill; a pass in football under pressure is an open skill requiring adaptation.

definition: Factors that prevent people from being physically active. Include: time constraints, cost, lack of facilities, social/cultural attitudes, low confidence, disability, and transport.
example: A single parent may cite lack of time and childcare as barriers to attending a gym.

definition: Regular physical activity reduces the risk of cardiovascular disease, Type 2 diabetes, obesity, osteoporosis, and mental health disorders (depression, anxiety). Improves mood via endorphin release.
example: 150 minutes of moderate aerobic activity per week (WHO guidelines) significantly reduces chronic disease risk.

definition: Participation in sport provides: social connections and friendships, sense of belonging, teamwork and communication skills, reduced isolation, and reduced antisocial behaviour.
example: A teenager joining a GAA club gains friends, learns teamwork, and has structured activity reducing idle time.

definition: The use of prohibited substances or methods to enhance athletic performance, banned by the World Anti-Doping Agency (WADA). Examples: anabolic steroids, EPO, blood doping, stimulants.
example: A cyclist using EPO (erythropoietin) to increase red blood cell count gains an aerobic advantage — but risks a 4-year ban.

definition: Organisations responsible for governing, developing, and promoting a specific sport in a country. Funded by Sport Ireland. Responsibilities: organising competitions, coach development, child safeguarding, anti-doping.
example: The GAA is the NGB for Gaelic games in Ireland — it organises All-Ireland championships and supports local clubs.
